title: The Angels' Song
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: The Angels' Song - Luke 2_1-14 - Pastor Tito Lyro.txt
outline: Introduction: The most majestic Christmas cantata vs. hallmark-card angels
God's sovereignty in Caesar's census directing Mary and Joseph to Bethlehem (vv.1-5)
The angels' audience: shepherds – despised and doubted in first-century society
Three things the angels' song communicates (v.14):
1. The nature of Christ's birth: the climax of divine self-revelation (Hebrews 1)
2. The significance of Christ's birth: peace through reconciliation with God
3. The consequence: God's abundant grace to those with whom He is pleased
Response of the shepherds: go, see, worship, and proclaim (vv.15-17)
summary: A Christmas sermon on Luke 2:1–14, expounding the one-line song of the heavenly host: 'Glory to God in the highest, and on earth peace, goodwill toward men.' Far from the decorative cherubs of Baroque paintings, these are the armies of God singing because the final revelation has come. The song's three clauses communicate: (1) the nature of Christ's birth as the ultimate self-revelation of God (Hebrews 1); (2) the significance of reconciliation and peace with God through the one Mediator; and (3) the consequence of God's grace poured out on His elect. The shepherds' response—go, see, worship, proclaim—models the proper response for all who hear the gospel.
transcript:
As a lot of you know, Emily grew up in the Tacoma Bible Presbyterian Church. For many years that church had a Christmas cantata on the Sunday closest to Christmas. They would put on a great production — an hour or more of singing. The choir would practice for weeks, serious practice, complicated songs, and they did a great job.

But all the years I attended the Christmas cantata at that church in Tacoma, none of them were like the one we read here in Luke chapter 2. Sure, that choir sang well. They sang elaborate, beautiful songs. But none of them compared to the glorious choir of angels in verse 14, singing one simple line: "Glory to God in the highest, and on earth peace, goodwill toward men."

Have you ever stopped to think about this scene seriously? Or does your mind go first to the Peanuts characters reciting it on their TV special? Let us set the scene as we move toward verse 14.

## Setting the Scene: God's Sovereignty in the Census (vv. 1–7)

Do you notice what is going on in verses 1 through 5? Here we have God guiding the heart of a wicked ruler — Caesar Augustus — arranging everything so that Mary and Joseph end up in Bethlehem to register for that census, which happened only about every fourteen years. God arranged all of this so that Jesus would be born in that little town of Bethlehem — no more than a village — in order to fulfill the great prophecy in Micah 5 concerning the Messiah of the Lord.

While they were there in Bethlehem to register for the census, Jesus arrives. You are very familiar with that story in verses 6 and 7.

## The Audience: Shepherds (vv. 8–9)

Following the birth of Jesus in that very humble condition — in a stable, or perhaps a cave where animals were kept — the most majestic Christmas cantata takes place in verses 8 through 14.

Do you notice the immediate audience of that cantata? The angels are singing to shepherds. That is an important detail, because shepherds were one of the lowest and most despised groups of people in first-century society. They had a reputation for being unreliable and dishonest — they struggled with telling the truth, and they tended to have problems respecting other people's property.

And yet that is precisely the audience to which the angels sing. This is not the group you would choose if you were trying to lend credibility to a story. If you were making something up and trying to gain credibility, you would have the angels appear to the king, or to synagogue rulers, or to the Pharisees. But they appear to shepherds.

The only reason the shepherds are included here is because what Luke is telling us actually happened. There is no other reason to include a discredited group in your account unless you are not trying to fabricate credibility — because what you wrote actually happened.

## The Heavenly Host (vv. 10–13)

To them the angels come to announce the birth of the Savior in verses 9 through 12. And as the angel finishes the announcement, all heaven breaks forth in praising God in verse 13 — much like the Hallelujah chorus in Revelation 19, related to the second coming of our Savior, where the hosts of heaven sing, "Hallelujah! Salvation and glory and honor and power belong to the Lord our God!"

If one angel was enough to cause the shepherds to be afraid, now the hosts of heaven appear. Luke says: "And suddenly there was with the angel a multitude of the heavenly hosts praising God and saying..."

The heavenly hosts — we are used to using that word and often don't know what it means. In the Bible, the hosts are the armies of God. We get our idea of angels from hallmark cards, or little decorations, or from Baroque paintings — perhaps cherubic, chubby little figures with tiny wings, blonde-haired and blue-eyed. But the Bible says these are the very soldiers of God who go about doing His bidding.

You may remember the story of Elisha. The Syrian army came to town, and as far as the eye could see, there were Syrian soldiers and chariots surrounding the city. The prophet's servant was afraid: "We're doomed!" But Elisha said, "I've got this — just trust me." He prayed that God would open the servant's eyes, and when the servant's eyes were opened, he saw: "Behold, the mountain was full of horses and chariots of fire all around Elisha."

Or read about the angels in Revelation 12 — they are not overweight children with little wings. They are the armies of heaven fighting for our very existence against the armies of Satan.

These are the ones singing about the birth of our Savior. We have this magnificent choir — the armies of God — singing praises because His Son has been born.

And the song is simple. One line. Two clauses that communicate three things:

> *"Glory to God in the highest, and on earth peace, goodwill toward men."* (Luke 2:14)

## I. The Nature of Christ's Birth (v. 14a)

The first thing this song communicates is the nature of the birth of Jesus. The coming of Christ is the climax of divine self-revelation. The angels are praising God because God has given to the world the final, full, and clear revelation of Himself.

God has revealed Himself in many different ways from the very beginning of time. Creation itself shouts His attributes. Psalm 19 tells us that "the heavens declare the glory of God, and the firmament shows His handiwork." Paul is even more precise in Romans 1:20: "For since the creation of the world, His invisible attributes are clearly seen, being understood by the things that are made, even His eternal power and Godhead, so that they are without excuse."

God has always been a God of revelation, a God who makes Himself known. But humanity has refused to listen to what creation declares about its Creator. So God started speaking in a special way — directly, or through messengers. He gave some people inspired visions that revealed what God was saying and thinking. He directed His people through His perfect law. He illustrated the need for atonement and forgiveness in the sacrificial system. He sent prophets to His people.

These were great and gracious revelations from God, but they were veiled. They were types and shadows of things to come. And so, 2,000 years ago, God gloriously sent His Son to make all things clear. That is why the author of Hebrews says:

> *"God, who at various times and in various ways spoke in time past to the fathers by the prophets, has in these last days spoken to us by His Son, whom He has appointed heir of all things, through whom also He made the worlds; who being the brightness of His glory and the express image of His person, and upholding all things by the word of His power, when He had by Himself purged our sins, sat down at the right hand of the Majesty on high."* (Hebrews 1:1–3)

In the Son, the fullness of God appears. That is why we don't need any more revelation. How could you improve on Jesus? You can't perfect the perfect. "For in Him dwells all the fullness of the Godhead bodily," Paul says. And the armies of heaven are praising God and declaring that all glory belongs to Him because the final revelation has come.

As John says:

> *"In the beginning was the Word, and the Word was with God, and the Word was God. He was in the beginning with God... And the Word became flesh and dwelt among us, and we beheld His glory, the glory as of the only begotten of the Father, full of grace and truth."* (John 1:1–2, 14)

This is the climax of all revelation. This is the climax of all communication from God — to the point that the armies of heaven cannot contain themselves and burst forth into singing.

## II. The Significance of Christ's Birth (v. 14b)

The second thing this song communicates is the significance of the birth of Jesus. To understand this correctly, we need to look at the wording of the second clause carefully. Our translation says: "and on earth peace, goodwill toward men." The English Standard Version does a better job of translating this clause: "and on earth peace among those with whom he is pleased."

The birth of Jesus is significant to the world because it is through Him that God and humanity are reconciled. That is the peace the angels are singing about.

The Apostle Paul says:

> *"Therefore, having been justified by faith, we have peace with God through our Lord Jesus Christ."* (Romans 5:1)

Christ Himself said:

> *"Peace I leave with you, My peace I give to you; not as the world gives do I give to you. Let not your heart be troubled, neither let it be afraid."* (John 14:27)

Brothers and sisters, friends, visitors — humanity was created to be in perfect union and peace with God. But we sinned, and that unity, that peace, was destroyed. Jesus Christ came to bring reconciliation between men and God.

Every last one of us in this room knows, deep in our hearts — whether you believe in Jesus Christ or not — that we are not at peace. If that is not the case, then why is it that society, humanity in general, is always searching for meaning, for fulfillment, for wholeness, for joy, for identity? Because they were created for those things. Because that union with God and that peace with God have been destroyed, our hearts are always yearning for them.

That is exactly what biblical peace is — a God-wrought wholeness, a God-wrought completeness. It is through this baby that the shepherds were about to meet that humanity and God can be reconciled. Paul says: "For there is one God and one Mediator between God and men, the Man Christ Jesus."

Paul writes in 2 Corinthians 5:

> *"Now all things are of God, who has reconciled us to Himself through Jesus Christ, and has given us the ministry of reconciliation, that is, that God was in Christ reconciling the world to Himself, not imputing their trespasses to them, and has committed to us the word of reconciliation. Now then, we are ambassadors for Christ, as though God were pleading through us: we implore you on Christ's behalf, be reconciled to God. For He made Him who knew no sin to be sin for us, that we might become the righteousness of God in Him."* (2 Corinthians 5:18–21)

This peace we all crave comes only through reconciliation with God, through the work of Christ. And it passes all understanding, as Paul tells us in Philippians 4. That is the only place you will find it.

## III. The Consequence: God's Grace to Those with Whom He Is Pleased (v. 14c)

The third thing this song communicates is the consequence of the birth of Jesus. The angels sing that peace is for those with whom He is pleased — those are the objects of God's grace. The grace of God is displayed to those who believe in Him. Through Jesus, the abundant grace of God is communicated to His elect.

In Titus 2, the apostle says: "For the grace of God that brings salvation has appeared to all men." This means that every one of you who does not believe in Jesus Christ is willingly refusing to see the grace of God that has been revealed to you.

I don't know if you have read *The Last Battle* — it is the final book in the Narnia series by C.S. Lewis. Near the end, some characters are sitting around, and the beauty of Narnia is all around them, but they are refusing to see it. They are insisting they are in a dark, stinking stable rather than in the paradise that is right in front of them. That is what people who refuse the grace of God displayed in the cross of Jesus Christ are doing — they are actively refusing to see what God has clearly displayed.

Titus 2 continues:

> *"...teaching us that, denying ungodliness and worldly lusts, we should live soberly, righteously, and godly in the present age, looking for the blessed hope and glorious appearing of our great God and Savior Jesus Christ, who gave Himself for us, that He might redeem us from every lawless deed and purify for Himself His own special people, zealous for good works."*

That is us — those who believe.

Do you believe that Jesus Christ came into the world in order to reconcile you to God and to grant you that fullness you so long for? If you do, then these words are true of you:

> *"For by grace you have been saved through faith, and that not of yourselves; it is the gift of God, not of works, lest anyone should boast. For we are His workmanship, created in Christ Jesus for good works, which God prepared beforehand that we should walk in them."* (Ephesians 2:8–10)

## Response: What Do We Do?

The Bible doesn't want you just to listen to things and say, "Deep. Good sermon." The Bible calls you to listen and to act. So what do we do with this? The shepherds show us.

Look at verse 15: "The shepherds said to one another, 'Let us now go to Bethlehem and see this thing that has come to pass, which the Lord has made known to us.'"

What do we do? We go see Jesus. We run to Him. We don't delay. If you have never done that, do it now. Trust in Him for the salvation of your soul. You are not guaranteed the next breath, so do it now.

But if you are already trusting in Christ, don't stop — keep going to Him for the constant peace that comes from Him. Run deeper into Him. Cling tighter to Him. Delight more strongly in Him.

And then worship Him. In verse 16, Luke tells us the shepherds came with haste and found Mary, Joseph, and the baby lying in the manger. They went and worshiped Jesus.

And then they could not contain themselves. What did they do next? In verse 17: they made widely known what they had seen and heard. We tell others about Jesus.

So do something with what you are hearing today. Believer or unbeliever, do something with what you hear here.

Glory to God in the highest, and on earth peace among those with whom He is pleased. That is the angels' song. Christ Jesus came to reconcile men to God.
